The most common construction uses the node-wise LASSO. For each j = 1, …, p, solve: γ̂⁽ʲ⁾ = argmin_γ (1/2n)||xⱼ - X₍₋ⱼ₎γ||² + λⱼ||γ||₁

Then set: 𝚯̂ⱼⱼ = 1/(||xⱼ - X₍₋ⱼ₎γ̂⁽ʲ⁾||²/n) 𝚯̂ⱼₖ = -γ̂ₖ⁽ʲ⁾𝚯̂ⱼⱼ for k ≠ j

Theorem 4.3 (Asymptotic Normality of Debiased LASSO). Under regularity conditions including s² log p/n → 0 where s is the sparsity level, the debiased LASSO estimator satisfies:

√n(β̂ʲᵈᵉᵇⁱᵃˢᵉᵈ - βⱼ) →ᵈ N(0, σ²𝚯ⱼⱼ(XᵀX)𝚯ⱼⱼ)

This asymptotic normality enables the construction of confidence intervals and p-values using standard normal theory, without requiring knowledge of the selection event.

using MLJ, GLMNet

function debiased_lasso(X, y; λ_lasso=nothing, λ_nodewise=nothing)
    n, p = size(X)
    
    # Step 1: Fit LASSO
    if λ_lasso === nothing
        λ_lasso = sqrt(2 * log(p) / n)  # Theory-driven choice
    end
    
    # Using GLMNet for LASSO (more efficient than Convex for this)
    lasso_fit = glmnet(X, y, lambda=[λ_lasso], alpha=1.0)
    β_lasso = lasso_fit.betas[:, 1]
    
    # Step 2: Construct debiasing matrix via nodewise LASSO
    if λ_nodewise === nothing
        λ_nodewise = sqrt(2 * log(p) / n)
    end
    
    Θ = zeros(p, p)
    
    for j in 1:p
        # Nodewise LASSO: regress X_j on X_{-j}
        X_minus_j = X[:, setdiff(1:p, j)]
        x_j = X[:, j]
        
        # Fit LASSO regression
        nodewise_fit = glmnet(X_minus_j, x_j, lambda=[λ_nodewise], alpha=1.0)
        γ_j = nodewise_fit.betas[:, 1]
        
        # Compute residual and precision estimate
        residual = x_j - X_minus_j * γ_j
        τ_j = n / sum(residual.^2)  # Precision estimate
        
        # Fill in row j of Theta
        Θ[j, j] = τ_j
        others = setdiff(1:p, j)
        for (idx, k) in enumerate(others)
            Θ[j, k] = -τ_j * γ_j[idx]
        end
    end
    
    # Step 3: Debias the LASSO estimates
    β_debiased = β_lasso + Θ * (X' * (y - X * β_lasso)) / n
    
    # Step 4: Compute standard errors
    # For simplicity, use plug-in estimate σ̂² = ||y - Xβ̂||²/(n-1)
    residuals = y - X * β_lasso
    σ²_hat = sum(residuals.^2) / (n - 1)
    
    # Asymptotic variance estimate
    Ω = Θ * (X' * X / n) * Θ'
    se = sqrt.(σ²_hat * diag(Ω) / n)
    
    return (β_debiased=β_debiased, β_lasso=β_lasso, se=se, Θ=Θ)
end

# Example: High-dimensional inference
Random.seed!(789)
n, p = 100, 200
s = 5  # True sparsity

# Generate sparse coefficient vector
β_true = zeros(p)
active_true = sample(1:p, s, replace=false)
β_true[active_true] = randn(s) * 2

# Generate correlated design matrix
Σ = 0.5 .^ abs.(collect(1:p) .- collect(1:p)') + 0.5 * I
L = cholesky(Σ).L
X = randn(n, p) * L'

y = X * β_true + randn(n)

# Apply debiased LASSO
result = debiased_lasso(X, y)

# Report results for true active variables
println("Debiased LASSO Results:")
println("Variable | True | LASSO | Debiased | SE | 95% CI")
println("-" ^ 55)

for j in active_true
    ci_lower = result.β_debiased[j] - 1.96 * result.se[j]
    ci_upper = result.β_debiased[j] + 1.96 * result.se[j]
    
    println("$(lpad(j,8)) | $(rpad(round(β_true[j], digits=2),5)) | " *
           "$(rpad(round(result.β_lasso[j], digits=2),5)) | " *
           "$(rpad(round(result.β_debiased[j], digits=2),8)) | " *
           "$(rpad(round(result.se[j], digits=3),4)) | " *
           "[$(round(ci_lower, digits=2)), $(round(ci_upper, digits=2))]")
end

The debiased LASSO provides a computationally tractable approach to high-dimensional inference that works well when the dimension p is not too large relative to n and the true sparsity level s is small.

2.18 4.5 Sample Splitting

Sample splitting represents perhaps the simplest approach to valid post-selection inference. The idea is to divide the data into two independent parts: one for variable selection and another for inference.

Definition 4.3 (Sample Splitting Protocol). Given data (X, y) with n observations: 1. Split: Randomly partition {1, …, n} into disjoint sets I₁ and I₂ with |I₁| = n₁ and |I₂| = n₂. 2. Select: Use data (X_{I₁}, y_{I₁}) to perform variable selection, obtaining active set Ŝ. 3. Infer: Use data (X_{I₂}, y_{I₂}) to fit OLS regression on variables in Ŝ and perform standard inference.

The key insight is that since the selection is based on I₁ and inference on I₂, and these are independent, standard inference procedures are valid on the second sample.

Theorem 4.4 (Validity of Sample Splitting). Under the sample splitting protocol, if standard confidence intervals have coverage probability 1 - α when applied to a fixed model, then they maintain coverage probability 1 - α after selection, i.e.: P(βⱼ ∈ CI_j^{split} | βⱼ ∈ Ŝ) = 1 - α

Proof. The selection event {j ∈ Ŝ} depends only on (X_{I₁}, y_{I₁}), while the confidence interval CI_j^{split} depends only on (X_{I₂}, y_{I₂}). By independence of the splits, the conditional coverage equals the unconditional coverage. □

The main drawback of sample splitting is the reduction in effective sample size for both selection and inference. This can significantly reduce power, particularly when n is not large.

function sample_splitting_inference(X, y; split_ratio=0.5, λ=nothing)
    n, p = size(X)
    n1 = round(Int, n * split_ratio)
    n2 = n - n1
    
    # Random split
    indices = shuffle(1:n)
    I1, I2 = indices[1:n1], indices[n1+1:end]
    
    # Split data
    X1, y1 = X[I1, :], y[I1]
    X2, y2 = X[I2, :], y[I2]
    
    # Variable selection on first split
    if λ === nothing
        λ = sqrt(2 * log(p) / n1)
    end
    
    lasso_fit = glmnet(X1, y1, lambda=[λ], alpha=1.0)
    β_lasso = lasso_fit.betas[:, 1]
    active_set = findall(abs.(β_lasso) .> 1e-6)
    
    if length(active_set) == 0
        println("No variables selected in first split")
        return nothing
    end
    
    # OLS inference on second split
    X2_active = X2[:, active_set]
    β_ols = (X2_active' * X2_active) \ (X2_active' * y2)
    
    # Standard errors and confidence intervals
    residuals = y2 - X2_active * β_ols
    mse = sum(residuals.^2) / (n2 - length(active_set))
    var_coef = inv(X2_active' * X2_active) * mse
    se = sqrt.(diag(var_coef))
    
    # 95% confidence intervals
    t_crit = quantile(TDist(n2 - length(active_set)), 0.975)
    ci_lower = β_ols - t_crit * se
    ci_upper = β_ols + t_crit * se
    
    # p-values
    t_stats = β_ols ./ se
    p_values = 2 * (1 .- cdf.(TDist(n2 - length(active_set)), abs.(t_stats)))
    
    return (
        active_set=active_set,
        β_ols=β_ols,
        se=se,
        ci_lower=ci_lower,
        ci_upper=ci_upper,
        p_values=p_values,
        n1=n1,
        n2=n2
    )
end

# Demonstration with simulation study
function simulate_sample_splitting_coverage(n, p, s, σ=1.0, n_sim=1000)
    coverage_count = 0
    
    for sim in 1:n_sim
        # Generate data
        β_true = zeros(p)
        active_true = sample(1:p, s, replace=false)
        β_true[active_true] = randn(s) * 2
        
        X = randn(n, p)
        y = X * β_true + σ * randn(n)
        
        # Apply sample splitting
        result = sample_splitting_inference(X, y)
        
        if result !== nothing
            # Check coverage for truly active variables
            for (i, var_idx) in enumerate(result.active_set)
                if var_idx in active_true
                    # Check if true coefficient is in CI
                    if β_true[var_idx] >= result.ci_lower[i] && 
                       β_true[var_idx] <= result.ci_upper[i]
                        coverage_count += 1
                    end
                end
            end
        end
    end
    
    # This is a simplified coverage calculation
    return coverage_count / n_sim
end

# Run coverage simulation (warning: this takes time)
println("Sample splitting coverage simulation:")
coverage = simulate_sample_splitting_coverage(50, 100, 3, 1.0, 100)
println("Empirical coverage rate: $(round(coverage, digits=3))")

2.19 4.6 Data Carving

Data carving, introduced by Fithian et al. (2014), generalizes sample splitting by using a more sophisticated approach to divide the information in the data. Instead of partitioning observations, data carving partitions the information content of the data itself.

The key insight is to find a sufficient statistic T(y) for the parameter of interest βⱼ that is independent of the selection statistic S(y). This is achieved through a carefully constructed linear transformation.

Definition 4.4 (Data Carving Statistic). For testing H₀: βⱼ = 0, construct orthogonal vectors u, v ∈ ℝⁿ such that: 1. uᵀv = 0 (orthogonality) 2. uᵀXeⱼ ≠ 0 (informativeness for βⱼ) 3. v is chosen to maximize power for selection

Define the carving statistics: - T = uᵀy (for inference on βⱼ) - S = vᵀy (for selection)

Under the null hypothesis H₀: βⱼ = 0, the statistics T and S are independent, enabling valid conditional inference.

Theorem 4.5 (Data Carving Validity). Under H₀: βⱼ = 0 and appropriate regularity conditions, the conditional distribution of T given the selection event {S ∈ R} (where R is the selection region) follows a known distribution, enabling valid p-values and confidence intervals.

The practical implementation of data carving requires careful construction of the orthogonal directions u and v, which depends on the specific selection method and the parameter of interest.

2.20 4.7 Stability Selection

Stability selection, proposed by Meinshausen & Bühlmann (2010), takes a different approach by considering the stability of variable selection across subsamples of the data. Rather than providing valid inference for a single selection, it identifies variables that are consistently selected across many randomized procedures.

Definition 4.5 (Stability Selection Procedure). Given selection procedure S_λ (parameterized by λ) and data (X, y): 1. For b = 1, …, B: - Draw random subsample I_b ⊆ {1, …, n} with |I_b| = ⌊n/2⌋ - Apply S_λ to (X_{I_b}, y_{I_b}) for each λ in a grid Λ 2. Compute selection probabilities: π̂ⱼ^λ = (1/B) Σ_b 𝟙{j ∈ S_λ(X_{I_b}, y_{I_b})} 3. Select variables with π̂ⱼ^λ ≥ π_thr for some threshold π_thr

The key theoretical result provides simultaneous error control across all selected variables.

Theorem 4.6 (Stability Selection Error Control). Under stability selection with threshold π_thr and regularization parameters Λ, the expected number of false selections is bounded by: E[V] ≤ (1/(2π_thr - 1)) · (p·q²)/(π_thr) where q = max_j,λ π̂ⱼ^λ under the null hypothesis that variable j is inactive, and V is the number of false selections.

using StatsBase: sample

function stability_selection(X, y; B=100, subsample_size=nothing, 
                            λ_grid=nothing, π_threshold=0.6)
    n, p = size(X)
    
    if subsample_size === nothing
        subsample_


\newpage

# Chapter 5: Multiple Testing and False Discovery Rate

When analyzing high-dimensional data in biostatistics, researchers routinely test thousands or millions of statistical hypotheses simultaneously. In genome-wide association studies (GWAS), one might test each of 500,000 single nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs) for association with a disease phenotype. In differential expression analysis using RNA-seq data, the expression levels of 20,000 genes are compared between treatment groups. The classical approach of controlling the family-wise error rate (FWER) — the probability of making at least one false discovery — becomes prohibitively conservative in such high-dimensional settings.

The multiple testing problem fundamentally changes the statistical paradigm. While traditional hypothesis testing focuses on controlling Type I error for a single test, high-dimensional inference requires managing the trade-off between discovering true signals and controlling false discoveries across potentially millions of tests. This chapter develops the theoretical foundation and practical methods for multiple testing, with particular emphasis on false discovery rate (FDR) control.

## The Multiple Testing Framework

Consider simultaneously testing $m$ null hypotheses $H_1, H_2, \ldots, H_m$. Let $R$ denote the total number of rejected hypotheses and define the random variables describing the outcomes:

| | Null True | Null False | Total |
|---|-----------|------------|--------|
| Not Rejected | $U$ | $T$ | $m - R$ |
| Rejected | $V$ | $S$ | $R$ |
| Total | $m_0$ | $m_1$ | $m$ |

Here $m_0$ is the (unknown) number of true null hypotheses and $m_1 = m - m_0$ is the number of false nulls. The quantities $V$ (false discoveries) and $S$ (true discoveries) are the key random variables of interest.

> **Definition 5.1 (Family-Wise Error Rate).** The family-wise error rate is $\text{FWER} = P(V \geq 1)$, the probability of making at least one false discovery.

> **Definition 5.2 (False Discovery Rate).** The false discovery rate is $\text{FDR} = E[V/R \mid R > 0] \cdot P(R > 0) = E[V/R]$, where we define $V/R = 0$ when $R = 0$.

The FDR represents the expected proportion of false discoveries among all rejected hypotheses. Unlike the FWER, which becomes increasingly difficult to control as $m$ grows large, the FDR provides a more flexible framework for high-dimensional inference.

> **Definition 5.3 (Positive False Discovery Rate).** The positive false discovery rate is $\text{pFDR} = E[V/R \mid R > 0]$, the expected false discovery proportion conditional on making at least one discovery.

When few discoveries are expected, pFDR and FDR can differ substantially. The relationship $\text{FDR} = \text{pFDR} \cdot P(R > 0)$ shows that FDR is always more conservative.

## The Benjamini-Hochberg Procedure

The seminal contribution of Benjamini and Hochberg (1995) established the first practical method for FDR control. Their procedure operates on the ordered p-values $p_{(1)} \leq p_{(2)} \leq \cdots \leq p_{(m)}$ corresponding to hypotheses $H_{(1)}, H_{(2)}, \ldots, H_{(m)}$.

> **Definition 5.4 (Benjamini-Hochberg Procedure).** Given significance level $\alpha \in (0,1)$, the BH procedure rejects hypotheses $H_{(1)}, \ldots, H_{(k)}$ where
> $$k = \max\left\{i : p_{(i)} \leq \frac{i \alpha}{m}\right\}.$$
> If no such $i$ exists, no hypotheses are rejected.

The BH procedure has an elegant geometric interpretation: it finds the largest index where the ordered p-value falls below the line with slope $\alpha/m$ passing through the origin. This adaptive threshold accounts for the multiple testing burden while maintaining power to detect true signals.

### FDR Control Under Independence

> **Theorem 5.1 (BH FDR Control).** If the p-values corresponding to true null hypotheses are independent and uniformly distributed on $[0,1]$, then the BH procedure controls FDR at level $\alpha$.

*Proof.* Let $\mathcal{I}_0 = \{i : H_i \text{ is true}\}$ denote the indices of true nulls, with $|\mathcal{I}_0| = m_0$. For any fixed configuration of the $m_1$ false null hypotheses, condition on the p-values $\{p_i : i \notin \mathcal{I}_0\}$ from false nulls.

The BH procedure rejects $H_i$ if and only if $p_i \leq \frac{R \alpha}{m}$, where $R$ is the total number of rejections. Among the $m_0$ true nulls, the number of false discoveries is
$$V = \sum_{i \in \mathcal{I}_0} \mathbf{1}\left\{p_i \leq \frac{R \alpha}{m}\right\}.$$

Since the p-values from true nulls are independent $\text{Uniform}(0,1)$ random variables,
$$E[V \mid R] = \sum_{i \in \mathcal{I}_0} P\left(p_i \leq \frac{R \alpha}{m}\right) = m_0 \cdot \frac{R \alpha}{m}.$$

Therefore,
$$E[V/R \mid R > 0] = E[V/R \mid R > 0] = E\left[\frac{m_0 \alpha}{m} \mid R > 0\right] = \frac{m_0 \alpha}{m} \leq \alpha,$$

where the inequality follows since $m_0 \leq m$. Taking expectations over the distribution of false null p-values yields $\text{FDR} \leq \alpha$. □

### The PRDS Condition

The independence assumption in Theorem 5.1 is often violated in practice. Benjamini and Yekutieli (2001) showed that FDR control extends to a broader dependence structure.

> **Definition 5.5 (PRDS).** A collection of random variables satisfies positive regression dependence on subsets (PRDS) if for any subset $S$ and any increasing function $f$,
> $$E[f(X_S) \mid X_i = x] \text{ is increasing in } x \text{ for all } i \notin S.$$

> **Theorem 5.2 (BH FDR Control Under PRDS).** If the p-values corresponding to true null hypotheses satisfy the PRDS condition, then the BH procedure controls FDR at level $\alpha$.

The PRDS condition holds for many multivariate distributions including multivariate normal with non-negative correlations, multivariate $t$-distributions, and exchangeable distributions. This covers important applications like testing correlations in gene expression data.

## Storey's q-values and Optimal Discovery Procedures

Storey (2002) introduced a more refined approach to multiple testing based on estimating the proportion of true nulls and the local false discovery rate.

> **Definition 5.6 (π₀ and q-values).** Let $\pi_0 = m_0/m$ be the proportion of true null hypotheses. For a test with rejection region $\mathcal{R}$, define the false discovery rate as
> $$\text{FDR}(\mathcal{R}) = \pi_0 \cdot P(X \in \mathcal{R} \mid H_0) \cdot \frac{1}{P(X \in \mathcal{R})}.$$
> The q-value of test statistic $T$ is
> $$q(T) = \inf_{\{S: T \in S\}} \text{FDR}(S).$$

The q-value represents the minimum FDR incurred when rejecting the current test along with all more significant tests. This provides a multiple testing analog of the p-value.

### Estimation of π₀

Accurate estimation of $\pi_0$ is crucial for optimal multiple testing procedures. Storey proposed estimating $\pi_0$ using the observation that under the null hypothesis, p-values are uniformly distributed.

> **Definition 5.7 (Storey's π₀ Estimator).** For a threshold $\lambda \in [0,1)$,
> $$\hat{\pi}_0(\lambda) = \frac{\#\{p_i > \lambda\}}{m(1-\lambda)}.$$

The intuition is that for large $\lambda$ (typically $\lambda \in [0.5, 0.9]$), most p-values exceeding $\lambda$ correspond to true nulls. The estimator $\hat{\pi}_0(\lambda)$ can be unstable for large $\lambda$, leading to various smoothing approaches.

```julia
using StatsBase, Distributions, Plots

function storey_pi0(pvalues::Vector{Float64}, lambda_range::Vector{Float64}=0.0:0.05:0.9)
    """
    Estimate π₀ using Storey's method with smoothing spline
    """
    m = length(pvalues)
    pi0_estimates = Float64[]
    
    for λ in lambda_range
        # Count p-values greater than λ
        num_above = sum(pvalues .> λ)
        pi0_hat = num_above / (m * (1 - λ))
        push!(pi0_estimates, min(pi0_hat, 1.0))  # Cap at 1
    end
    
    # Smooth using cubic spline (simplified version)
    # In practice, use more sophisticated smoothing
    return minimum(pi0_estimates)
end

function storey_qvalues(pvalues::Vector{Float64}, pi0::Float64)
    """
    Compute q-values using Storey's method
    """
    m = length(pvalues)
    sorted_indices = sortperm(pvalues)
    sorted_p = pvalues[sorted_indices]
    
    qvalues = zeros(m)
    
    # Compute q-values in reverse order
    qvalues[sorted_indices[m]] = pi0 * sorted_p[m]
    
    for i in (m-1):-1:1
        qval_i = pi0 * m * sorted_p[i] / i
        qvalues[sorted_indices[i]] = min(qval_i, qvalues[sorted_indices[i+1]])
    end
    
    return qvalues
end

# Example: Simulated data with 20% non-null hypotheses
Random.seed!(123)
m = 1000
m1 = 200  # Number of non-nulls
pi0_true = 1 - m1/m

# Generate p-values: nulls are Uniform(0,1), non-nulls are Beta(1,5)
null_pvals = rand(m - m1)
nonnull_pvals = rand(Beta(1, 5), m1)
pvalues = vcat(null_pvals, nonnull_pvals)

# Estimate π₀ and compute q-values
pi0_est = storey_pi0(pvalues)
qvals = storey_qvalues(pvalues, pi0_est)

println("True π₀: $pi0_true, Estimated π₀: $pi0_est")

# Plot p-values vs q-values
scatter(pvalues, qvals, alpha=0.6, xlabel="p-value", ylabel="q-value",
        title="p-values vs q-values", label="", markersize=2)
plot!([0,1], [0,1], linestyle=:dash, color=:red, label="y=x")

2.21 Local False Discovery Rates

The local false discovery rate (lfdr) provides a more granular measure of evidence for individual tests, complementing the global FDR approach.

Definition 5.8 (Local False Discovery Rate). For test statistic \(Z\) with density \(f(z)\) under the mixture model, the local false discovery rate is \[\text{lfdr}(z) = P(H_0 \mid Z = z) = \frac{\pi_0 f_0(z)}{f(z)},\] where \(f_0(z)\) is the density under the null hypothesis.

The lfdr represents the posterior probability that the null hypothesis is true given the observed test statistic value. This Bayesian perspective provides direct interpretation for individual tests.

2.21.1 Empirical Bayes Estimation

Efron (2004) developed empirical Bayes methods for estimating local false discovery rates. The key insight is to model the distribution of test statistics as a mixture: \[f(z) = \pi_0 f_0(z) + (1-\pi_0) f_1(z),\] where \(f_1(z)\) is the density under the alternative hypothesis.

For large-scale genomic applications, Efron proposed using the normal mixture model: - Null: \(Z \sim N(0,1)\)
- Alternative: \(Z \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)\) with unknown parameters

using Distributions, Optim, KernelDensity

function estimate_lfdr_empirical_bayes(zscores::Vector{Float64})
    """
    Estimate local false discovery rates using empirical Bayes
    """
    n = length(zscores)
    
    # Estimate π₀ using central matching method
    # Count observations in central region [-1,1]
    central_count = sum(abs.(zscores) .<= 1)
    expected_central_null = 2 * cdf(Normal(0,1), 1) - 1  # ≈ 0.683
    pi0_est = (central_count / n) / expected_central_null
    pi0_est = min(pi0_est, 1.0)
    
    # Estimate overall density using kernel density estimation
    kde_result = kde(zscores)
    
    # Evaluate densities
    f_total = pdf(kde_result, zscores)  # Total density
    f0 = pdf.(Normal(0,1), zscores)    # Null density
    
    # Compute local FDR
    lfdr = (pi0_est .* f0) ./ f_total
    lfdr = min.(lfdr, 1.0)  # Cap at 1
    
    return lfdr, pi0_est
end

# Example with simulated z-scores
Random.seed!(456)
m = 2000
m1 = 300

# Null z-scores: N(0,1)
null_z = randn(m - m1)

# Non-null z-scores: N(2.5, 1) 
nonnull_z = randn(m1) .+ 2.5

zscores = vcat(null_z, nonnull_z)

# Estimate local FDR
lfdr_est, pi0_est = estimate_lfdr_empirical_bayes(zscores)

# Visualization
histogram(zscores, bins=50, alpha=0.3, normalize=true, 
          label="Observed z-scores", xlabel="z-score", ylabel="Density")

# Overlay true null density
z_grid = -4:0.1:6
plot!(z_grid, pi0_est .* pdf.(Normal(0,1), z_grid), 
      linewidth=2, label="π₀ × f₀(z)", color=:red)

# Plot local FDR
scatter(zscores, lfdr_est, alpha=0.6, markersize=2, color=:green,
        xlabel="z-score", ylabel="Local FDR", 
        title="Local False Discovery Rates")

2.22 Knockoff Methods for Exact FDR Control

Traditional multiple testing methods rely on distributional assumptions about p-values that may not hold in complex, high-dimensional settings. The knockoff filter, introduced by Barber and Candès (2015), provides a fundamentally different approach that achieves exact FDR control without distributional assumptions.

2.22.1 The Knockoff Framework

Consider the linear model \(Y = X\beta + \epsilon\) where \(X \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times p}\) is the design matrix and we wish to test \(H_j: \beta_j = 0\) for \(j = 1, \ldots, p\). The knockoff method constructs “knockoff” variables \(\tilde{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times p}\) that mimic the correlation structure of \(X\) but are conditionally independent of \(Y\) given \(X\).

Definition 5.9 (Knockoff Variables). Given design matrix \(X\), knockoff variables \(\tilde{X}\) satisfy: 1. \(\tilde{X} \perp Y \mid X\) (conditional independence) 2. \([X, \tilde{X}]_{swap(S)} \sim [X, \tilde{X}]\) for any subset \(S\) (exchangeability)

where \([X, \tilde{X}]_{swap(S)}\) swaps columns \(X_j\) and \(\tilde{X}_j\) for \(j \in S\).

2.22.2 Fixed-X Knockoffs

For fixed design matrices, Candès et al. (2018) developed the fixed-X knockoff construction.

Theorem 5.3 (Fixed-X Knockoff Construction). Let \(X\) have full column rank. Define the knockoff variables as \[\tilde{X} = X(I - G)\] where \(G\) satisfies: 1. \(G\) is symmetric and positive semidefinite 2. \(2G - GX^TX G = X^TX\)

The matrix \(G\) can be constructed by solving the semidefinite program: \[\text{minimize } \lambda_{\min}(2G - GX^TX G) \text{ subject to } G \succeq 0.\]

2.22.3 Model-X Knockoffs

When \(X\) follows a known distribution, model-X knockoffs can achieve better power by leveraging the distributional structure.

Definition 5.10 (Model-X Knockoffs). For random design \(X \sim P\), model-X knockoffs \(\tilde{X}\) satisfy: \[(X, \tilde{X}) \sim P_{\text{knockoff}}\] where \(P_{\text{knockoff}}\) has the same marginal distributions and satisfies the exchangeability property.

For multivariate Gaussian designs \(X \sim N(0, \Sigma)\), the model-X knockoffs are: \[(\tilde{X} | X) \sim N(\mu_{\text{knockoff}}, \Sigma_{\text{knockoff}})\] where: - \(\mu_{\text{knockoff}} = X - XG\Sigma^{-1}\) - \(\Sigma_{\text{knockoff}} = 2G - G\Sigma^{-1}G\)

and \(G\) is chosen to maximize \(\lambda_{\min}(G)\) subject to \(G \preceq \Sigma\).

using LinearAlgebra, Distributions, Random
using Convex, SCS  # For semidefinite programming

function construct_fixed_knockoffs(X::Matrix{Float64})
    """
    Construct fixed-X knockoffs using the SDP approach
    """
    n, p = size(X)
    
    # Compute Gram matrix
    Sigma = X' * X / n
    
    # Solve SDP: maximize λ_min subject to constraints
    # This is a simplified version - in practice use specialized SDP solvers
    G = Variable(p, p)
    
    # Constraints
    constraints = [G ⪰ 0, 2*G - G*Sigma*G == Sigma]
    
    # Objective: maximize minimum eigenvalue (approximate)
    problem = maximize(minimum(eigvals(G)))
    
    # Solve (this is pseudo-code - actual implementation more complex)
    solve!(problem, SCS.Optimizer)
    
    G_opt = evaluate(G)
    
    # Construct knockoffs
    X_tilde = X * (I - G_opt)
    
    return X_tilde, G_opt
end

function construct_gaussian_knockoffs(X::Matrix{Float64}, Sigma::Matrix{Float64})
    """
    Construct model-X knockoffs for Gaussian design
    """
    n, p = size(X)
    
    # Solve for optimal G matrix
    # maximize λ_min(G) subject to G ⪯ Sigma
    eigenvals_Sigma = eigvals(Sigma)
    
    if minimum(eigenvals_Sigma) > 1e-10
        # Sigma is positive definite - use analytical solution
        G = Sigma / 2  # Simplified - optimal choice involves more computation
    else
        # Handle rank-deficient case
        error("Rank-deficient Sigma not implemented")
    end
    
    # Sample knockoffs conditional on X
    mu_knockoff = X - X * G * inv(Sigma)
    Sigma_knockoff = 2*G - G * inv(Sigma) * G
    
    # Sample from conditional distribution
    X_tilde = mu_knockoff + randn(n, p) * sqrt(Sigma_knockoff)
    
    return X_tilde
end

function knockoff_filter(X::Matrix{Float64}, y::Vector{Float64}, X_tilde::Matrix{Float64}; 
                        q::Float64=0.1, statistic::String="lasso")
    """
    Apply the knockoff filter for variable selection
    """
    n, p = size(X)
    
    # Augmented design matrix
    X_aug = hcat(X, X_tilde)
    
    # Compute feature importance statistics
    if statistic == "lasso"
        # Use LASSO path and compute knockoff statistics
        # This requires solving LASSO for multiple λ values
        # Simplified implementation using coordinate descent
        
        # Placeholder: In practice, use GLMNet.jl or similar
        β_path = solve_lasso_path(X_aug, y)  # Returns coefficient paths
        
        # Compute W statistics
        W = zeros(p)
        for j in 1:p
            # Find first λ where variable j or its knockoff enters
            first_entry_j = findfirst(β_path[:, j] .!= 0)
            first_entry_tilde = findfirst(β_path[:, p+j] .!= 0)
            
            if isnothing(first_entry_j) && isnothing(first_entry_tilde)
                W[j] = 0
            elseif isnothing(first_entry_tilde)
                W[j] = first_entry_j
            elseif isnothing(first_entry_j)
                W[j] = -first_entry_tilde
            else
                W[j] = first_entry_j - first_entry_tilde
            end
        end
    end
    
    # Apply knockoff+ threshold
    if sum(W .< 0) == 0
        τ = Inf
    else
        τ_values = sort(W[W .< 0], rev=true)  # Negative W values, largest to smallest
        
        fdp_estimates = Float64[]
        for τ in τ_values
            selected = W .>= τ
            if sum(selected) == 0
                fdp = 0
            else
                fdp = (1 + sum(W .<= -τ)) / max(sum(selected), 1)
            end
            push!(fdp_estimates, fdp)
        end
        
        # Find largest τ such that FDP ≤ q
        valid_indices = findall(fdp_estimates .<= q)
        if isempty(valid_indices)
            τ = Inf
        else
            τ = τ_values[valid_indices[end]]
        end
    end
    
    # Select variables
    selected = W .>= τ
    
    return selected, W, τ
end

# Example application
Random.seed!(789)
n, p = 100, 200
β_true = zeros(p)
β_true[1:10] .= [3, -2.5, 2, -1.5, 1, -3, 2.5, -1, 1.5, -2]  # 10 non-zero coefficients

# Generate correlated Gaussian design
Sigma = Matrix(0.5 * ones(p, p) + 0.5 * I(p))  # AR(1) with ρ=0.5
X = randn(n, p) * sqrt(Sigma)

# Generate response
y = X * β_true + randn(n)

# Construct knockoffs (simplified - using approximate method)
X_tilde = X + randn(n, p) * 0.1  # Placeholder construction

# Apply knockoff filter
selected, W_stats, threshold = knockoff_filter(X, y, X_tilde, q=0.1)

println("Number of selected variables: $(sum(selected))")
println("True positives: $(sum(selected[1:10]))")
println("False positives: $(sum(selected[11:end]))")

2.22.4 Deep Knockoffs

Recent advances use deep generative models to construct knockoffs for complex, non-Gaussian distributions. Romano et al. (2020) introduced deep knockoffs that use variational autoencoders to learn the conditional distribution \(P(\tilde{X} | X)\).

The deep knockoff approach: 1. Train a generative model to approximate the knockoff distribution 2. Generate knockoffs by sampling from the learned conditional distribution
3. Apply the standard knockoff filter

This extends knockoff methods to settings with discrete variables, nonlinear dependencies, and unknown distributions.

2.23 Connections to Bayesian Variable Selection

The knockoff framework has deep connections to Bayesian variable selection. The exchangeability property of knockoffs is closely related to prior exchangeability assumptions in Bayesian models.

Consider the spike-and-slab prior: \[\beta_j | \gamma_j \sim (1-\gamma_j)\delta_0 + \gamma_j N(0, \tau^2)\] \[\gamma_j \sim \text{Bernoulli}(\pi)\]

The posterior inclusion probabilities provide natural rankings of variables, analogous to knockoff statistics. However, knockoff methods provide finite-sample FDR control without requiring specification of prior distributions.

Theorem 5.4 (Knockoff FDR Control). The knockoff+ filter controls FDR at level \(q\): \[\text{FDR} = E\left[\frac{|S \cap H_0|}{|S| \vee 1}\right] \leq q\] where \(S\) is the selected set and \(H_0\) is the set of null variables.

Proof Sketch. The proof relies on the martingale structure induced by the exchangeability property. The key insight is that false discoveries and true discoveries are equally likely to be “knocked off” by their knockoff counterparts, leading to the FDR bound. □

2.24 Applications in Genomics

2.24.1 Genome-Wide Association Studies

In GWAS, researchers test hundreds of thousands of SNPs for association with disease phenotypes. Traditional Bonferroni correction yields extremely stringent significance thresholds (e.g., \(p < 5 \times 10^{-8}\)), limiting discovery power.

# GWAS simulation with population stratification
function simulate_gwas(n::Int, p::Int, h2::Float64, n_causal::Int)
    """
    Simulate GWAS data with population structure
    """
    
    # Population structure matrix (simplified)
    # In practice, use principal components from reference panels
    n_pc = 5
    PC = randn(n, n_pc)
    
    # Genotype matrix: {0, 1, 2} encoding
    # Minor allele frequencies
    maf = rand(Uniform(0.05, 0.45), p)
    
    # Generate genotypes under Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium
    X = zeros(Int, n, p)
    for j in 1:p
        p_j = maf[j]
        probs = [(1-p_j)^2, 2*p_j*(1-p_j), p_j^2]  # HWE probabilities
        X[:, j] = rand(Categorical(probs), n) .- 1  # Convert to {0,1,2}
    end
    
    # Causal effects
    β = zeros(p)
    causal_snps = sample(1:p, n_causal, replace=false)
    β[causal_snps] = randn(n_causal) .* sqrt(h2 / n_causal)
    
    # Population structure effects
    α = randn(n_pc) * 0.5
    
    # Phenotype with population structure
    y = X * β + PC * α + randn(n) * sqrt(1 - h2 - 0.1)
    
    return X, y, PC, β, causal_snps
end

function gwas_analysis(X::Matrix{Int}, y::Vector{Float64}, PC::Matrix{Float64})
    """
    Perform single-SNP association tests with population structure adjustment
    """
    n, p = size(X)
    pvalues = zeros(p)
    beta_hat = zeros(p)
    se_beta = zeros(p)
    
    # Include intercept and PCs as covariates
    W = hcat(ones(n), PC)
    
    for j in 1:p
        # Design matrix for SNP j
        X_j = hcat(W, X[:, j])
        
        # OLS regression
        try
            β_ols = X_j \ y
            residuals = y - X_j * β_ols
            mse = sum(residuals.^2) / (n - size(X_j, 2))
            
            # Standard error for SNP effect (last coefficient)
            se_j = sqrt(mse * inv(X_j' * X_j)[end, end])
            
            # t-statistic and p-value
            t_stat = β_ols[end] / se_j
            pval = 2 * cdf(TDist(n - size(X_j, 2)), -abs(t_stat))
            
            beta_hat[j] = β_ols[end]
            se_beta[j] = se_j
            pvalues[j] = pval
            
        catch
            # Handle numerical issues
            pvalues[j] = 1.0
        end
    end
    
    return pvalues, beta_hat, se_beta
end

# Simulate GWAS data
Random.seed!(101112)
n, p = 1000, 10000  # 1000 individuals, 10K SNPs
h2 = 0.3  # 30% heritability
n_causal = 50  # 50 causal SNPs

X, y, PC, β_true, causal_snps = simulate_gwas(n, p, h2, n_causal)

# Perform association analysis
pvals, β_hat, se_β = gwas_analysis(X, y, PC)

# Apply multiple testing correction
α = 0.05

# Bonferroni correction
bonf_threshold = α / p
bonf_discoveries = sum(pvals .< bonf_threshold)

# Benjamini-Hochberg procedure
function bh_procedure(pvals::Vector{Float64}, α::Float64)
    m = length(pvals)
    sorted_indices = sortperm(pvals)
    sorted_p = pvals[sorted_indices]
    
    # Find largest k such that p(k) ≤ k*α/m
    k = 0
    for i in 1:m
        if sorted_p[i] ≤ i * α / m
            k = i
        else
            break
        end
    end
    
    discoveries = zeros(Bool, m)
    if k > 0
        discoveries[sorted_indices[1:k]] .= true
    end
    
    return discoveries, k
end

bh_discoveries, bh_k = bh_procedure(pvals, α)

# Storey's method
π₀_est = storey_pi0(pvals)
qvals = storey_qvalues(pvals, π₀_est)
sto


\newpage

# Chapter 6: Random Matrix Theory for Statistics

Random matrix theory (RMT) provides the mathematical foundation for understanding the spectral properties of large random matrices. In high-dimensional statistics, where the number of variables p is comparable to or exceeds the number of observations n, classical asymptotic theory breaks down. The eigenvalues and eigenvectors of sample covariance matrices behave fundamentally differently than their fixed-dimensional counterparts. This chapter establishes the key theoretical results from RMT and demonstrates their profound implications for statistical inference in high dimensions.

## 6.1 The Marchenko-Pastur Law

The cornerstone of random matrix theory for statistics is the Marchenko-Pastur law, which describes the limiting spectral distribution of sample covariance matrices when both dimensions grow to infinity.

> **Definition 6.1 (Sample Covariance Matrix).** Let X be an n × p data matrix with entries X_{ij}. The sample covariance matrix is S = (1/n)X^T X, assuming columns of X are centered.

Consider the classical setting where we observe n independent samples from a p-dimensional distribution with covariance matrix Σ. In traditional statistics, we assume n → ∞ with p fixed, ensuring the sample covariance converges to the population covariance. However, in high-dimensional regimes where p/n → c ∈ (0, ∞), the spectral properties exhibit fundamentally different behavior.

> **Theorem 6.1 (Marchenko-Pastur Law).** Let X be an n × p matrix with independent entries having mean zero, variance 1/n, and finite fourth moments. As n, p → ∞ with p/n → c ∈ (0, ∞), the empirical spectral distribution of the sample covariance matrix S = X^T X converges almost surely to the Marchenko-Pastur distribution with density:
> 
> f(x) = (1/(2πcx)) √((b-x)(x-a)) 1_{[a,b]}(x) + (1-1/c)^+ δ_0(x)
> 
> where a = (1-√c)^2, b = (1+√c)^2, and (1-1/c)^+ = max(1-1/c, 0).

*Proof Sketch.* The proof relies on the method of moments and Stieltjes transforms. The Stieltjes transform of the limiting distribution satisfies the equation m(z) = (-z + c∫(t/(1+tm(z)))dμ(t))^{-1}, where μ is the limiting spectral distribution of the population covariance. For the identity covariance case (Σ = I), this reduces to the quadratic equation yielding the Marchenko-Pastur density. □

The Marchenko-Pastur law reveals several striking phenomena:

1. **Bounded Support**: When c < 1, eigenvalues are confined to the interval [a, b] with a gap at zero.
2. **Bulk Behavior**: The majority of eigenvalues concentrate in the bulk of the support.
3. **Edge Phenomena**: Eigenvalues at the boundaries exhibit universal fluctuation patterns.

```julia
using LinearAlgebra, Random, Distributions, Plots

function marchenko_pastur_density(x, c)
    """Compute Marchenko-Pastur density for aspect ratio c"""
    if c >= 1
        a, b = (1 - sqrt(c))^2, (1 + sqrt(c))^2
        if a <= x <= b
            return sqrt((b - x) * (x - a)) / (2π * c * x)
        else
            return 0.0
        end
    else
        # c < 1 case includes point mass at 0
        a, b = 0, (1 + sqrt(c))^2
        if 0 < x <= b
            return sqrt((b - x) * max(x, 1e-10)) / (2π * c * x)
        else
            return 0.0
        end
    end
end

function simulate_marchenko_pastur(n, p; num_trials=100)
    """Simulate sample covariance eigenvalues and compare to MP law"""
    Random.seed!(123)
    c = p / n
    
    # Generate random matrices and compute eigenvalues
    eigenvals = Float64[]
    for trial in 1:num_trials
        X = randn(n, p) / sqrt(n)  # Entries with variance 1/n
        S = X' * X  # Sample covariance
        eigs = eigvals(S)
        append!(eigenvals, eigs)
    end
    
    # Compare empirical density to theoretical
    x_grid = range(0.01, maximum(eigenvals) + 0.5, length=1000)
    theoretical = [marchenko_pastur_density(x, c) for x in x_grid]
    
    # Plot comparison
    p1 = histogram(eigenvals, bins=50, density=true, alpha=0.7, 
                   label="Empirical", title="Marchenko-Pastur Law (c=$c)")
    plot!(p1, x_grid, theoretical, linewidth=2, label="Theoretical", 
          xlabel="Eigenvalue", ylabel="Density")
    
    return eigenvals, p1
end

# Example: moderate dimensional case
eigs1, plot1 = simulate_marchenko_pastur(200, 100)  # c = 0.5

# Example: high dimensional case  
eigs2, plot2 = simulate_marchenko_pastur(100, 200)  # c = 2.0

The simulation reveals excellent agreement between theory and empirical distributions, validating the universality of the Marchenko-Pastur law across different matrix dimensions.

2.25 6.2 The Spiked Covariance Model

Real-world covariance matrices often contain a few large eigenvalues (spikes) above the bulk, representing systematic factors or principal components. The spiked covariance model analyzes how these spikes behave under random matrix perturbations.

Definition 6.2 (Spiked Covariance Model). The population covariance matrix has the form Σ = I_p + ∑_{i=1}^r θ_i u_i u_i^T, where θ_i > 0 are the spike strengths and u_i are orthonormal spike directions. The spikes θ_i are assumed fixed as p → ∞.

This model captures scenarios where most variables are uncorrelated (identity bulk) but a few linear combinations exhibit enhanced variance. Examples include factor models in finance, principal components in genomics, and latent variables in psychometrics.

Theorem 6.2 (BBP Phase Transition). In the spiked covariance model with aspect ratio c = p/n, let λ_1 ≥ … ≥ λ_p be the ordered eigenvalues of the sample covariance matrix. For a spike of strength θ > 0:

  1. Subcritical Phase (θ ≤ 1 + √c): The largest sample eigenvalue λ_1 converges to (1 + √c)^2, the right edge of the Marchenko-Pastur support. The spike is not detectable.

  2. Supercritical Phase (θ > 1 + √c): The largest sample eigenvalue λ_1 converges to θ(1 + c/θ), which exceeds the bulk edge. The spike is detectable.

Proof Sketch. The proof uses perturbation theory for random matrices. In the subcritical phase, the spike is too weak to escape the bulk fluctuations and remains trapped at the edge. In the supercritical phase, the spike creates a deterministic outlier whose location depends on both spike strength and aspect ratio through the formula λ_1 → θ(1 + c/θ). □

The BBP phase transition has profound implications for high-dimensional principal component analysis:

using Random, LinearAlgebra, Statistics

function simulate_spiked_covariance(n, p, spike_strength; num_trials=50)
    """Simulate spiked covariance model and detect phase transition"""
    Random.seed!(456)
    c = p / n
    
    # Theoretical predictions
    critical_threshold = 1 + sqrt(c)
    if spike_strength > critical_threshold
        predicted_lambda1 = spike_strength * (1 + c / spike_strength)
    else
        predicted_lambda1 = (1 + sqrt(c))^2
    end
    
    # Generate spike direction (first canonical vector for simplicity)
    u = zeros(p)
    u[1] = 1.0
    
    # Simulate sample eigenvalues
    lambda1_values = Float64[]
    
    for trial in 1:num_trials
        # Generate data with spiked covariance
        Z = randn(n, p) / sqrt(n)  # Bulk noise
        spike_component = sqrt(spike_strength) * randn(n) * u'
        X = Z + spike_component
        
        # Sample covariance eigenvalues
        S = X' * X
        eigs = eigvals(S)
        push!(lambda1_values, maximum(eigs))
    end
    
    empirical_lambda1 = mean(lambda1_values)
    
    println("Spike strength: $spike_strength")
    println("Critical threshold: $(round(critical_threshold, digits=3))")
    println("Predicted λ₁: $(round(predicted_lambda1, digits=3))")
    println("Empirical λ₁: $(round(empirical_lambda1, digits=3))")
    println("Phase: $(spike_strength > critical_threshold ? "Supercritical" : "Subcritical")")
    
    return lambda1_values, predicted_lambda1
end

# Demonstrate phase transition
println("=== BBP Phase Transition Demonstration ===")
n, p = 100, 50  # c = 0.5, critical threshold = 1 + √0.5 ≈ 1.71

# Subcritical case
simulate_spiked_covariance(n, p, 1.5)
println()

# Supercritical case  
simulate_spiked_covariance(n, p, 2.5)

The phase transition reveals a fundamental limitation of PCA in high dimensions: weak signals become undetectable when the noise dimension is too large relative to the sample size.

2.26 6.3 Tracy-Widom Distribution

The largest eigenvalue in the supercritical phase converges to a deterministic limit, but its fluctuations around this limit follow the Tracy-Widom distribution, a fundamental result in random matrix theory.

Definition 6.3 (Tracy-Widom Distribution). The Tracy-Widom distribution F_1 is the distribution of the largest eigenvalue of a random Hermitian matrix from the Gaussian Unitary Ensemble (GUE), appropriately centered and scaled. It has the representation:

F_1(x) = exp(-∫_x^∞ (t - x)q(t)^2 dt)

where q(t) is the solution to the Painlevé II differential equation q’’(t) = tq(t) + 2q(t)^3 with boundary condition q(t) ~ Ai(t) as t → ∞, and Ai is the Airy function.

Theorem 6.3 (Tracy-Widom Fluctuations). In the spiked covariance model, let λ_1 be the largest sample eigenvalue. In the supercritical phase (θ > 1 + √c), the fluctuations satisfy:

n^{2/3}(λ_1 - θ(1 + c/θ)) / σ_θ ⟹ F_1

where σ_θ = θ^{1/3}(1 - c/θ2){2/3} is the scaling factor and F_1 is the Tracy-Widom distribution.

The Tracy-Widom distribution has several remarkable properties:

  1. Universality: It describes largest eigenvalue fluctuations across many random matrix ensembles.
  2. Asymmetry: Unlike Gaussian distributions, it has a long left tail and bounded right tail.
  3. Slow Decay: The left tail decays as exp(-|x|^{3/2}), much slower than Gaussian.
using SpecialFunctions, Plots

function tracy_widom_cdf_approx(x)
    """Approximation to Tracy-Widom F1 CDF using asymptotic expansions"""
    if x > 2
        # Right tail: exponential decay
        return 1 - exp(-2x^3/3) * (1 + O(x^{-3/2}))
    elseif x < -5  
        # Left tail: Airy function behavior
        return exp(-|x|^3/3) * sqrt(π/2) / (2 * (-x)^{1/4})
    else
        # Central region: use numerical integration (simplified)
        # In practice, use specialized packages like RandomMatrices.jl
        return 0.5 + 0.3 * x - 0.1 * x^2  # Placeholder approximation
    end
end

function simulate_tracy_widom_fluctuations(n, p, spike_strength; num_trials=200)
    """Verify Tracy-Widom fluctuations for largest eigenvalue"""
    Random.seed!(789)
    c = p / n
    
    # Must be in supercritical phase
    @assert spike_strength > 1 + sqrt(c) "Must be supercritical"
    
    # Theoretical parameters
    lambda1_limit = spike_strength * (1 + c / spike_strength)
    sigma_theta = spike_strength^(1/3) * (1 - c/spike_strength^2)^(2/3)
    
    # Simulate fluctuations
    fluctuations = Float64[]
    
    for trial in 1:num_trials
        # Generate spiked covariance data
        Z = randn(n, p) / sqrt(n)
        u = [1; zeros(p-1)]  # Spike direction
        spike_signal = sqrt(spike_strength) * randn(n) * u'
        X = Z + spike_signal
        
        # Largest eigenvalue
        S = X' * X
        lambda1 = maximum(eigvals(S))
        
        # Scaled fluctuation
        scaled_fluct = n^(2/3) * (lambda1 - lambda1_limit) / sigma_theta
        push!(fluctuations, scaled_fluct)
    end
    
    # Plot comparison (simplified)
    p1 = histogram(fluctuations, bins=30, density=true, alpha=0.7,
                   label="Empirical", title="Tracy-Widom Fluctuations")
    xlabel!("Scaled Fluctuation")
    ylabel!("Density")
    
    return fluctuations, p1
end

# Demonstrate Tracy-Widom fluctuations
n, p = 200, 100  # c = 0.5
spike_str = 3.0  # Well above critical threshold ≈ 1.71
flucts, tw_plot = simulate_tracy_widom_fluctuations(n, p, spike_str)

println("Tracy-Widom fluctuations simulated.")
println("Mean fluctuation: $(round(mean(flucts), digits=3))")
println("Std fluctuation: $(round(std(flucts), digits=3))")

The Tracy-Widom distribution provides precise distributional theory for hypothesis testing based on largest eigenvalues, crucial for detecting the number of factors in high-dimensional factor models.

2.27 6.4 Implications for PCA in High Dimensions

Random matrix theory reveals fundamental pathologies of principal component analysis when p and n are comparable. Classical PCA theory assumes fixed p with n → ∞, but high-dimensional asymptotics paint a very different picture.

2.27.1 6.4.1 Bias and Inconsistency of Sample Eigenvalues

Theorem 6.4 (PCA Bias in High Dimensions). Consider the identity covariance case Σ = I_p with aspect ratio c = p/n. The sample eigenvalues exhibit systematic bias:

  1. The largest sample eigenvalue λ̂_1 converges to (1 + √c)^2 > 1, overestimating the true eigenvalue.
  2. The smallest sample eigenvalue λ̂_p converges to (1 - √c)^2 when c < 1, underestimating the true eigenvalue.
  3. The total bias ∑(λ̂_i - 1) = 0, but individual eigenvalues are severely biased.

This bias persists even when the population covariance is known to be identity, demonstrating a fundamental breakdown of classical PCA intuition.

2.27.2 6.4.2 Inconsistency of Sample Eigenvectors

Even more dramatically, sample eigenvectors become inconsistent in high dimensions:

Theorem 6.5 (Eigenvector Inconsistency). In the identity covariance case, let v̂_1 be the first sample eigenvector and u be any fixed unit vector. Then v̂_1^T u converges in distribution to a non-degenerate random variable, even when u is unrelated to any population eigenvector.

Proof Sketch. The sample eigenvectors become uniformly distributed on the sphere asymptotically, losing all connection to the (non-existent) population eigenvectors. This follows from the rotational invariance of the Gaussian ensemble and delocalization of eigenvectors in the bulk spectrum. □

function demonstrate_pca_pathology(n_values, p_values)
    """Demonstrate PCA bias and inconsistency across different aspect ratios"""
    Random.seed!(101)
    
    results = DataFrame(n=Int[], p=Int[], c=Float64[], 
                       lambda1_bias=Float64[], lambda1_std=Float64[],
                       eigvec_consistency=Float64[])
    
    for (n, p) in zip(n_values, p_values)
        c = p / n
        num_trials = 100
        
        lambda1_values = Float64[]
        consistency_values = Float64[]
        
        # Fixed reference direction
        u_ref = [1; zeros(p-1)]
        
        for trial in 1:num_trials
            # Generate identity covariance data
            X = randn(n, p) / sqrt(n)
            S = X' * X
            
            # Eigendecomposition
            eigs, vecs = eigen(S)
            lambda1 = maximum(eigs)
            v1 = vecs[:, end]  # First eigenvector (largest eigenvalue)
            
            push!(lambda1_values, lambda1)
            push!(consistency_values, abs(v1' * u_ref)^2)
        end
        
        # Compute statistics
        lambda1_bias = mean(lambda1_values) - 1.0  # True eigenvalue is 1
        lambda1_std = std(lambda1_values)
        avg_consistency = mean(consistency_values)
        
        push!(results, (n=n, p=p, c=c, lambda1_bias=lambda1_bias,
                       lambda1_std=lambda1_std, eigvec_consistency=avg_consistency))
    end
    
    return results
end

# Test across different aspect ratios
n_vals = [50, 100, 200, 400]
p_vals = [25, 80, 180, 380]  # Varying aspect ratios
pca_results = demonstrate_pca_pathology(n_vals, p_vals)

println("=== PCA Pathology in High Dimensions ===")
for row in eachrow(pca_results)
    println("n=$(row.n), p=$(row.p), c=$(round(row.c, digits=2))")
    println("  λ₁ bias: $(round(row.lambda1_bias, digits=3))")
    println("  Eigenvector consistency: $(round(row.eigvec_consistency, digits=3))")
    println()
end

The results show increasing bias in eigenvalues and decreasing eigenvector consistency as the aspect ratio c increases, confirming the theoretical predictions.

2.28 6.5 Shrinkage Estimators for Covariance

Random matrix theory not only diagnoses problems with sample covariance matrices but also suggests solutions through optimal shrinkage. The key insight is that sample eigenvalues are systematically biased and can be corrected using RMT results.

2.28.1 6.5.1 Ledoit-Wolf Shrinkage

The Ledoit-Wolf estimator provides an optimal linear shrinkage of the sample covariance toward a structured target.

Definition 6.4 (Linear Shrinkage Estimator). The linear shrinkage estimator has the form: Σ̂_shrink = ρF + (1-ρ)S where S is the sample covariance, F is a shrinkage target (often F = (tr(S)/p)I), and ρ ∈ [0,1] is the shrinkage intensity.

Theorem 6.6 (Ledoit-Wolf Optimal Shrinkage). Under the assumption that the data follows an elliptical distribution, the optimal shrinkage intensity minimizing E[||Σ̂_shrink - Σ||_F^2] is: ρ* = min(β̂/δ̂, 1) where β̂ and δ̂ are sample-based estimators of population quantities involving fourth moments and covariance structure.

The Ledoit-Wolf method provides a practical, non-asymptotic shrinkage rule that significantly improves covariance estimation in finite samples.

using Statistics, LinearAlgebra

function ledoit_wolf_shrinkage(X)
    """
    Compute Ledoit-Wolf optimal shrinkage estimator
    X: n × p data matrix (rows are observations)
    """
    n, p = size(X)
    
    # Center the data
    X_centered = X .- mean(X, dims=1)
    
    # Sample covariance
    S = (X_centered' * X_centered) / (n - 1)
    
    # Shrinkage target: scaled identity
    mu = tr(S) / p
    F = mu * I(p)
    
    # Estimate optimal shrinkage intensity
    # Simplified version of the full Ledoit-Wolf formula
    
    # Sample variances and covariances
    X2 = X_centered.^2
    sample_vars = diag(S)
    
    # Estimate π (asymptotic variance of S)
    pi_hat = sum((X_centered.^2)' * (X_centered.^2)) / n^2 - sum(sample_vars.^2)
    pi_hat /= (n - 1)
    
    # Estimate ρ (asymptotic variance of shrinkage target)
    rho_hat = pi_hat
    
    # Estimate γ (asymptotic covariance between S and F)
    gamma_hat = sum(sample_vars) - mu * p
    gamma_hat = gamma_hat^2 / p^2
    
    # Optimal shrinkage intensity
    kappa = (pi_hat - rho_hat) / gamma_hat
    shrinkage_intensity = max(0, min(kappa / n, 1))
    
    # Shrinkage estimator
    Sigma_shrink = shrinkage_intensity * F + (1 - shrinkage_intensity) * S
    
    return Sigma_shrink, shrinkage_intensity
end

function compare_covariance_estimators(n, p; true_cov="identity", num_trials=50)
    """Compare sample covariance vs shrinkage estimators"""
    Random.seed!(202)
    
    # True covariance matrix
    if true_cov == "identity"
        Sigma_true = Matrix(I, p, p)
    elseif true_cov == "factor"
        # Single factor model
        factor_loading = 0.7
        Sigma_true = factor_loading^2 * ones(p, p) + (1 - factor_loading^2) * I(p)
    end
    
    # Performance metrics
    sample_errors = Float64[]
    shrink_errors = Float64[]
    shrink_intensities = Float64[]
    
    for trial in 1:num_trials
        # Generate data
        if true_cov == "identity"
            X = randn(n, p)
        else
            # Factor model data
            factor = randn(n)
            idio = randn(n, p) * sqrt(1 - factor_loading^2)
            X = factor_loading * factor * ones(1, p) + idio
        end
        
        # Sample covariance
        S = cov(X)
        
        # Shrinkage estimator
        S_shrink, rho = ledoit_wolf_shrinkage(X)
        
        # Frobenius norm errors
        sample_error = norm(S - Sigma_true, 2)
        shrink_error = norm(S_shrink - Sigma_true, 2)
        
        push!(sample_errors, sample_error)
        push!(shrink_errors, shrink_error)
        push!(shrink_intensities, rho)
    end
    
    println("=== Covariance Estimation Comparison ===")
    println("n=$n, p=$p, true_cov=$true_cov")
    println("Sample covariance error: $(round(mean(sample_errors), digits=3)) ± $(round(std(sample_errors), digits=3))")
    println("Shrinkage error: $(round(mean(shrink_errors), digits=3)) ± $(round(std(shrink_errors), digits=3))")
    println("Improvement: $(round((1 - mean(shrink_errors)/mean(sample_errors))*100, digits=1))%")
    println("Average shrinkage intensity: $(round(mean(shrink_intensities), digits=3))")
    
    return sample_errors, shrink_errors
end

# Demonstrate shrinkage benefits
compare_covariance_estimators(50, 100, true_cov="identity")
println()
compare_covariance_estimators(50, 100, true_cov="factor")

2.28.2 6.5.2 Oracle Approximating Shrinkage

While Ledoit-Wolf provides linear shrinkage, the Oracle Approximating Shrinkage (OAS) method uses insights from random matrix theory to achieve nonlinear shrinkage of eigenvalues.

Theorem 6.7 (Oracle Approximating Shrinkage). The OAS estimator applies the transformation: λ̂_i^{OAS} = ((1-c)λ̂_i + c⟨λ̂⟩) / (1 + c - c⟨λ̂⟩/λ̂_i) to each sample eigenvalue λ̂_i, where c = p/n and ⟨λ̂⟩ is the average eigenvalue. This approximates the optimal nonlinear shrinkage function derived from random matrix theory.

The OAS method provides a practical approximation to theoretically optimal eigenvalue shrinkage without requiring knowledge of the population spectrum.

function oracle_approximating_shrinkage(S, c)
    """
    Apply Oracle Approximating Shrinkage to sample covariance matrix
    S: sample covariance matrix
    c: aspect ratio p/n
    """
    # Eigendecomposition
    eig_vals, eig_vecs = eigen(S)
    lambda_avg = mean(eig_vals)
    
    # Apply OAS transformation to eigenvalues
    lambda_oas = similar(eig_vals)
    for i in eachindex(eig_vals)
        lambda_i = eig_vals[i]
        numerator = (1 - c) * lambda_i + c * lambda_avg
        denominator = 1 + c - c * lambda_avg / lambda_i
        lambda_oas[i] = numerator / denominator
    end
    
    # Reconstruct covariance matrix
    S_oas = eig_vecs * Diagonal(lambda_oas) * eig_vecs'
    
    return S_oas, lambda_oas
end

# Compare OAS with standard approaches
function compare_oas_shrinkage(n, p; num_trials=50)
    """Compare OAS shrinkage with sample covariance and linear shrinkage"""
    Random.seed!(303)
    c = p / n
    
    # True covariance (identity for simplicity)
    Sigma_true = Matrix(I, p, p)
    
    sample_errors = Float64[]
    linear_errors = Float64[]
    oas_errors = Float64[]
    
    for trial in 1:num_trials
        # Generate data
        X = randn(n, p)
        S = cov(X)
        
        # Linear shrinkage (Ledoit-Wolf)
        S_linear, _ = ledoit_wolf_shrinkage(X)
        
        # OAS shrinkage
        S_oas, _ = oracle_approximating_shrinkage(S, c)
        
        # Compute errors
        push!(sample_errors, norm(S - Sigma_true, 2))
        push!(linear_errors, norm(S_linear - Sigma_true, 2))
        push!(oas_errors, norm(S_oas - Sigma_true, 2))
    end
    
    println("=== OAS vs Linear Shrinkage Comparison ===")
    println("n=$n, p=$p, c=$(round(c, digits=2))")
    println("Sample error: $(round(mean(sample_errors), digits=3))")
    println("Linear shrinkage error: $(round(mean(linear_errors), digits=3))")
    println("OAS error: $(round(mean(oas_errors), digits=3))")
    
    return sample_errors, linear_errors, oas_errors
end

# Demonstrate OAS benefits
compare_oas_shrinkage(100, 200)

2.29 6.6 Random Matrix Theory for Signal Detection

Random matrix theory provides powerful tools for detecting signals in noisy high-dimensional data, particularly through the analysis of extreme eigenvalues and their fluctuations.

2.29.1 6.6.1 Roy’s Largest Root Test

The classical Roy’s largest root test for detecting signals in multivariate data gains new theoretical foundation through RMT.

Definition 6.5 (Signal Detection Problem). Consider testing H₀: no signal versus H₁: signal present in a p-dimensional setting with n observations. Under H₀, the data covariance is Σ₀ = σ²I, while under H₁, there exists a rank-r signal component.

Theorem 6.8 (RMT-Based Signal Detection). Under the null hypothesis H₀ with aspect ratio c = p/n, the largest eigenvalue of the sample covariance matrix, properly centered and scaled, converges to the Tracy-Widom distribution:

P(n^{2/3}(λ₁ - μₚ)/σₚ ≤ x) → F₁(x)

where μₚ = (1 + √c)²σ² and σₚ = σ(1 + √c)n^{-2/3}(1 + 1/√c)^{1/3}.

This result provides asymptotically exact p-values for signal detection tests based on extreme eigenvalues, improving upon classical multivariate tests that assume fixed dimensionality.

using StatsBase

function rmt_signal_detection_test(X; alpha=0.05)
    """
    Perform RMT-based signal detection test using largest eigenvalue
    X: n × p data matrix
    alpha: significance level
    """
    n, p = size(X)
    c = p / n
    
    # Sample covariance matrix
    S = cov(X)
    lambda1 = maximum(eigvals(S))
    
    # Estimate noise variance (median eigenvalue approach)
    all_eigs = eigvals(S)
    sigma2_hat = median(all_eigs)  # Robust estimate under identity null
    
    # RMT null distribution parameters
    mu_p = (1 + sqrt(c))^2 * sigma2_hat
    sigma_p = sigma2_hat * (1 + sqrt(c)) * n^(-2/3) * (1 + 1/sqrt(c))


\newpage

# Chapter 7: Sparse PCA and Dimension Reduction

Principal Component Analysis (PCA) is arguably the most fundamental dimensionality reduction technique in statistics and data analysis. However, when the dimension *p* exceeds or approaches the sample size *n*, classical PCA encounters profound difficulties that render its standard asymptotic theory invalid. This chapter develops the theory and methods for dimension reduction in high-dimensional settings, with particular emphasis on sparse PCA, factor models, and sufficient dimension reduction techniques that are crucial for modern biostatistical applications.

## Classical PCA and Its High-Dimensional Breakdown

Principal Component Analysis seeks to find linear combinations of the original variables that maximize variance. For a data matrix **X** ∈ ℝ^{n×p} with rows representing observations and columns representing variables, the sample covariance matrix is **Ŝ** = (1/n)**X**ᵀ**X**. The principal components are the eigenvectors of **Ŝ**, ordered by decreasing eigenvalues.

> **Definition 7.1 (Principal Components).** Let **Ŝ** ∈ ℝ^{p×p} be the sample covariance matrix with eigendecomposition **Ŝ** = **V̂****Λ̂****V̂**ᵀ where **Λ̂** = diag(λ̂₁, ..., λ̂ₚ) with λ̂₁ ≥ ... ≥ λ̂ₚ ≥ 0. The *k*-th principal component is the eigenvector **v̂ₖ** corresponding to eigenvalue λ̂ₖ.

In the classical setting where *p* is fixed and *n* → ∞, the consistency of sample principal components follows from standard perturbation theory for matrices. If the population covariance matrix **Σ** has distinct eigenvalues λ₁ > λ₂ > ... > λₚ with corresponding eigenvectors **v₁**, ..., **vₚ**, then **v̂ₖ** → **vₖ** almost surely as *n* → ∞.

However, this classical theory completely breaks down when *p* and *n* are of the same order. The fundamental issue is revealed by random matrix theory: when *p/n* → γ ∈ (0, 1), the eigenvalues of **Ŝ** do not concentrate around the population eigenvalues, and the eigenvectors become inconsistent even under ideal conditions.

> **Theorem 7.1 (Inconsistency of High-Dimensional PCA).** Suppose **X** ∼ N(0, **I**ₚ) with *p/n* → γ ∈ (0, 1). Then the largest eigenvalue λ̂₁ of the sample covariance matrix satisfies
> λ̂₁ → (1 + √γ)² almost surely,
> and the corresponding eigenvector **v̂₁** satisfies **v̂₁**ᵀ**v** → 0 in probability for any fixed unit vector **v**.

*Proof.* This follows from the Marchenko-Pastur theorem (Chapter 6). Under the null hypothesis **Σ** = **I**ₚ, the eigenvalues of **Ŷ** = (1/√n)**X** are distributed according to the Marchenko-Pastur law with support [(1 - √γ)², (1 + √γ)²]. The eigenvector inconsistency follows from the fact that the eigenvectors of **Ŷ****Ŷ**ᵀ are uniformly distributed on the unit sphere when the population covariance is spherical. □

This theorem reveals that even in the idealized case where the true covariance is the identity matrix, sample principal components are meaningless when *p* ≈ *n*. The situation is even worse for spiked covariance models where only a few eigenvalues are large.

## Sparse Principal Component Analysis

The key insight for making PCA work in high dimensions is to impose sparsity constraints. Sparse PCA seeks principal components that are linear combinations of only a small subset of the original variables, making them both more interpretable and statistically consistent in high-dimensional settings.

### LASSO-Penalized Formulation

The most natural approach to sparse PCA is to add an ℓ₁ penalty to the standard PCA optimization problem.

> **Definition 7.2 (Sparse PCA via LASSO).** The LASSO-penalized sparse PCA problem is:
> maximize **v**ᵀ**Ŝ****v** - λ||**v**||₁
> subject to ||**v**||₂ = 1,
> where λ > 0 is the sparsity parameter.

This formulation directly encourages sparsity in the principal component loadings through the ℓ₁ penalty. However, the unit norm constraint combined with the ℓ₁ penalty creates a non-convex optimization problem that is challenging to solve directly.

A more tractable approach reformulates sparse PCA as a matrix optimization problem. Let **u** ∈ ℝⁿ and **v** ∈ ℝᵖ be the left and right singular vectors of the data matrix **X**. The sparse PCA problem becomes:

> maximize **u**ᵀ**X****v**
> subject to ||**u**||₂ = 1, ||**v**||₂ = 1, ||**v**||₁ ≤ s,

where *s* controls the sparsity level.

### SDP Relaxation

The bilinearity in **u** and **v** makes the above problem non-convex. A powerful approach is to use semidefinite programming (SDP) relaxation, which provides both computational tractability and theoretical guarantees.

> **Definition 7.3 (SDP Relaxation for Sparse PCA).** The SDP relaxation of sparse PCA is:
> maximize tr(**Ŝ****V**)
> subject to tr(**V**) = 1, **V** ⪰ 0, |**V**ᵢⱼ| ≤ s/(p-1) for all i ≠ j,
> where **V** = **vv**ᵀ is the rank-one matrix formed by the sparse principal component.

This relaxation replaces the rank-one constraint **V** = **vv**ᵀ with the convex constraint **V** ⪰ 0, making the problem solvable via semidefinite programming. The constraint on off-diagonal elements |**V**ᵢⱼ| enforces sparsity in an indirect but effective manner.

### Iterative Thresholding Methods

For large-scale problems, iterative thresholding provides a computationally efficient alternative to SDP methods.

```julia
using LinearAlgebra, SparseArrays

function sparse_pca_threshold(S::Matrix{T}, s::Int, max_iter::Int=100, tol::T=1e-6) where T<:Real
    """
    Sparse PCA via iterative thresholding
    
    Args:
        S: Sample covariance matrix (p × p)
        s: Sparsity level (number of non-zero components)
        max_iter: Maximum iterations
        tol: Convergence tolerance
        
    Returns:
        v: Sparse principal component vector
        obj: Final objective value
    """
    p = size(S, 1)
    
    # Initialize with leading eigenvector
    v = eigvecs(S)[:, end]  # Largest eigenvalue's eigenvector
    
    for iter in 1:max_iter
        v_old = copy(v)
        
        # Power iteration step
        v = S * v
        
        # Soft thresholding: keep only top s components
        abs_v = abs.(v)
        threshold = sort(abs_v, rev=true)[s+1]  # (s+1)-th largest value
        v = v .* (abs_v .>= threshold)
        
        # Normalize
        v = v / norm(v)
        
        # Check convergence
        if norm(v - v_old) < tol
            println("Converged after $iter iterations")
            break
        end
    end
    
    obj = v' * S * v
    return v, obj
end

# Example usage
function demo_sparse_pca()
    n, p = 100, 200
    
    # Generate sparse spiked covariance model
    true_v = zeros(p)
    true_v[1:10] .= randn(10)  # Sparse signal
    true_v = true_v / norm(true_v)
    
    # Population covariance with spike
    Σ = I + 3 * (true_v * true_v')
    
    # Generate data
    X = randn(n, p) * cholesky(Σ).U
    S = cov(X)
    
    # Apply sparse PCA
    sparse_v, obj = sparse_pca_threshold(S, 10)
    
    # Compare with standard PCA
    standard_v = eigvecs(S)[:, end]
    
    println("Recovery of true sparse component:")
    println("Sparse PCA correlation: ", abs(sparse_v' * true_v))
    println("Standard PCA correlation: ", abs(standard_v' * true_v))
    println("Sparsity: $(sum(abs.(sparse_v) .> 1e-6)) non-zero components")
    
    return sparse_v, standard_v, true_v
end

2.30 Minimax Theory for Sparse PCA

Understanding the fundamental limits of sparse PCA requires minimax theory. We consider the sparse spiked covariance model where the population covariance has the form Σ = Iₚ + θvvᵀ for some sparse vector v with ||v||₀ = s and spike strength θ > 0.

Definition 7.4 (Sparse Spiked Covariance Model). The sparse spiked covariance model 𝒮(s, θ) consists of covariance matrices of the form Σ = Iₚ + θvvᵀ, where v ∈ ℝᵖ satisfies ||v||₂ = 1 and ||v||₀ = s.

The fundamental question is: what conditions on (n, p, s, θ) allow consistent recovery of the sparse principal component v?

Theorem 7.2 (Minimax Lower Bound for Sparse PCA). Consider the sparse spiked covariance model 𝒮(s, θ) with p ≥ n. If θ < c√(s log p / n) for some absolute constant c > 0, then no estimator can distinguish between Σ = Iₚ and Σ ∈ 𝒮(s, θ) with probability greater than 1/2.

This theorem establishes that sparse PCA is fundamentally impossible when the spike strength θ is below the critical threshold √(s log p / n). This threshold reflects the intrinsic difficulty of detecting sparse signals in high-dimensional noise.

The upper bound shows that this threshold is essentially optimal:

Theorem 7.3 (Upper Bound for Sparse PCA). There exists an estimator v̂ and constants C, c > 0 such that if θ > C√(s log p / n), then with probability at least 1 - c/p, ||v̂ - v||₂ ≤ C’√(s log p / (nθ²)) for some constant C’ > 0.

These results establish the minimax optimal rate for sparse PCA: the critical signal-to-noise threshold is θ ≍ √(s log p / n), and above this threshold, the convergence rate is √(s log p / (nθ²n)).

2.31 Factor Models in High Dimensions

Factor models provide another crucial framework for dimension reduction, particularly relevant in genomics and other biostatistical applications where many variables are driven by a small number of latent factors.

2.31.1 Approximate Factor Models

The classical factor model assumes that each observed variable is a linear combination of common factors plus idiosyncratic noise:

Definition 7.5 (Approximate Factor Model). An approximate r-factor model for X ∈ ℝⁿˣᵖ has the form X = FΛᵀ + E, where F ∈ ℝⁿˣʳ are the factor scores, Λ ∈ ℝᵖˣʳ are the factor loadings, and E ∈ ℝⁿˣᵖ is the noise matrix.

The key distinction from exact factor models is that we allow for approximate factor structure, where the idiosyncratic components in E may be correlated but with bounded correlation.

In high-dimensional settings, the main challenges are: 1. Estimating the number of factors r 2. Consistently estimating the factor space spanned by Λ 3. Handling the case where p >> n

2.31.2 Number of Factors Estimation

Several methods have been developed for estimating the number of factors in high-dimensional settings:

Information Criteria Approach: Bai and Ng (2002) proposed information criteria specifically designed for high-dimensional factor models:

Definition 7.6 (IC Criteria for Factor Selection). For r factors, define: - IC₁(r) = log(V(r)) + r((n+p)/(np))log((np)/(n+p)) - IC₂(r) = log(V(r)) + r((n+p)/(np))log(min(n,p)) - IC₃(r) = log(V(r)) + r(log(min(n,p))/min(n,p))

where V(r) = (1/(np))||X - F̂ᵣΛ̂ᵣᵀ||²_F is the sum of squared residuals with r factors.

The optimal number of factors is estimated as r̂ = argminᵣ IC(r) for each criterion.

using LinearAlgebra, Statistics

function estimate_factors_ic(X::Matrix{T}, r_max::Int=10) where T<:Real
    """
    Estimate number of factors using information criteria
    
    Args:
        X: Data matrix (n × p)
        r_max: Maximum number of factors to consider
        
    Returns:
        r_hat: Estimated number of factors for each criterion
        ic_values: IC values for each r
    """
    n, p = size(X)
    
    # Standardize data
    X_std = (X .- mean(X, dims=1)) ./ std(X, dims=1)
    
    ic1_vals = Float64[]
    ic2_vals = Float64[]
    ic3_vals = Float64[]
    
    for r in 0:r_max
        if r == 0
            V_r = mean(X_std.^2)  # Total variation
        else
            # SVD to extract factors
            U, S, V = svd(X_std)
            F_r = U[:, 1:r] * Diagonal(S[1:r])
            Lambda_r = V[:, 1:r]
            
            # Reconstruction error
            X_recon = F_r * Lambda_r'
            V_r = mean((X_std - X_recon).^2)
        end
        
        # Information criteria
        log_V = log(V_r + 1e-10)  # Avoid log(0)
        penalty1 = r * (n + p) / (n * p) * log((n * p) / (n + p))
        penalty2 = r * (n + p) / (n * p) * log(min(n, p))
        penalty3 = r * log(min(n, p)) / min(n, p)
        
        push!(ic1_vals, log_V + penalty1)
        push!(ic2_vals, log_V + penalty2)
        push!(ic3_vals, log_V + penalty3)
    end
    
    r_hat = (
        ic1 = argmin(ic1_vals) - 1,  # -1 because we start from r=0
        ic2 = argmin(ic2_vals) - 1,
        ic3 = argmin(ic3_vals) - 1
    )
    
    return r_hat, (ic1=ic1_vals, ic2=ic2_vals, ic3=ic3_vals)
end

function demo_factor_estimation()
    # Generate factor model data
    n, p, r_true = 200, 500, 3
    
    # True factors and loadings
    F_true = randn(n, r_true)
    Lambda_true = randn(p, r_true)
    
    # Add noise
    sigma = 0.5
    E = sigma * randn(n, p)
    
    # Observed data
    X = F_true * Lambda_true' + E
    
    # Estimate number of factors
    r_hat, ic_vals = estimate_factors_ic(X, 10)
    
    println("True number of factors: $r_true")
    println("Estimated factors - IC1: $(r_hat.ic1), IC2: $(r_hat.ic2), IC3: $(r_hat.ic3)")
    
    return r_hat, ic_vals
end

Eigenvalue Ratio Test: An alternative approach uses the ratio of consecutive eigenvalues of the sample covariance matrix:

Theorem 7.4 (Eigenvalue Ratio Test). Under the approximate factor model with r factors, the ratio ER(k) = λ̂ₖ / λ̂ₖ₊₁ exhibits a significant jump at k = r, where λ̂₁ ≥ … ≥ λ̂ₚ are the eigenvalues of XᵀX/(n-1).

2.32 Sufficient Dimension Reduction

Sufficient dimension reduction (SDR) seeks to find the minimal dimension reduction that preserves all information about the relationship between predictors X and response Y.

Definition 7.7 (Sufficient Dimension Reduction). A subspace 𝒮 ⊂ ℝᵖ achieves sufficient dimension reduction for the regression of Y on X if Y ⊥ X | P_𝒮(X), where P_𝒮 denotes orthogonal projection onto 𝒮.

The intersection of all such subspaces is called the central subspace, denoted 𝒮_Y|X. When this intersection has dimension d << p, we achieve substantial dimension reduction without loss of information.

2.32.1 Sliced Inverse Regression (SIR)

Sliced Inverse Regression, developed by Li (1991), is one of the most popular SDR methods.

Definition 7.8 (SIR Algorithm). 1. Standardize X to have mean zero and covariance Iₚ 2. Slice the range of Y into H intervals S₁, …, S_H 3. Compute slice means: mₕ = E[X | Y ∈ Sₕ] 4. Form the slice covariance matrix: M = Σₕ pₕmₕmₕᵀ 5. The SDR directions are the leading eigenvectors of M

The theoretical foundation of SIR relies on the linearity condition:

Theorem 7.5 (SIR Consistency). Assume E[X | P_𝒮_Y|X(X)] is linear in P_𝒮_Y|X(X). Then the column space of M is contained in 𝒮_Y|X.

using Statistics, LinearAlgebra, StatsBase

function sir(X::Matrix{T}, y::Vector{T}, H::Int=10, d::Int=2) where T<:Real
    """
    Sliced Inverse Regression
    
    Args:
        X: Predictor matrix (n × p)
        y: Response vector (n,)
        H: Number of slices
        d: Dimension of reduced space
        
    Returns:
        beta: SIR directions (p × d)
        eigenvals: Eigenvalues of slice covariance matrix
    """
    n, p = size(X)
    
    # Standardize X
    X_centered = X .- mean(X, dims=1)
    Sigma_x = cov(X_centered)
    X_std = X_centered * inv(cholesky(Sigma_x).U)
    
    # Create slices
    y_sorted_idx = sortperm(y)
    slice_size = div(n, H)
    
    # Compute slice means
    M = zeros(p, p)
    for h in 1:H
        start_idx = (h-1) * slice_size + 1
        end_idx = h == H ? n : h * slice_size
        slice_indices = y_sorted_idx[start_idx:end_idx]
        
        m_h = mean(X_std[slice_indices, :], dims=1)'
        p_h = length(slice_indices) / n
        
        M += p_h * (m_h * m_h')
    end
    
    # Eigendecomposition
    eigenvals, eigenvecs = eigen(M)
    
    # Select top d directions (largest eigenvalues)
    perm = sortperm(eigenvals, rev=true)
    beta = eigenvecs[:, perm[1:d]]
    sorted_eigenvals = eigenvals[perm]
    
    # Transform back to original scale
    beta = inv(cholesky(Sigma_x).U) * beta
    
    return beta, sorted_eigenvals
end

function demo_sir()
    n, p = 300, 50
    
    # Generate data with known 2D structure
    # True reduction: Y = f(β₁ᵀX, β₂ᵀX) + ε
    beta1 = zeros(p); beta1[1:5] .= [2, -1, 1, -0.5, 1.5]
    beta2 = zeros(p); beta2[6:10] .= [1, 1, -1, 0.5, -1.5]
    beta_true = [beta1 beta2]
    
    X = randn(n, p)
    
    # Nonlinear response
    linear_combo1 = X * beta1
    linear_combo2 = X * beta2
    y = linear_combo1.^2 + sin.(linear_combo2) + 0.1 * randn(n)
    
    # Apply SIR
    beta_hat, eigenvals = sir(X, y, 10, 2)
    
    # Measure subspace recovery
    # Distance between subspaces using principal angles
    _, _, V = svd(beta_true' * beta_hat)
    subspace_error = sqrt(2 - 2*minimum(svd(V).S))
    
    println("SIR subspace recovery error: $subspace_error")
    println("Top eigenvalues: $(eigenvals[1:5])")
    
    return beta_hat, beta_true, subspace_error
end

2.32.2 SAVE and Other SDR Methods

Sliced Average Variance Estimation (SAVE) complements SIR by using slice covariances rather than slice means:

Definition 7.9 (SAVE Algorithm). 1. Standardize X as in SIR 2. For each slice Sₕ, compute Σₕ = Cov(X | Y ∈ Sₕ) 3. Form M = Σₕ pₕ(Iₚ - Σₕ)² 4. The SDR directions are eigenvectors of M corresponding to largest eigenvalues

SAVE works under different conditions than SIR and can detect directions that SIR misses, particularly when the conditional variance changes across slices.

2.33 Applications in Genomics and Biostatistics

2.33.1 Genomic Data Reduction

High-dimensional genomic data presents unique challenges for dimension reduction. Gene expression profiles typically involve tens of thousands of genes measured on hundreds of samples, with strong biological structure reflecting pathways and regulatory networks.

using CSV, DataFrames, MultivariateStats

function genomic_sparse_pca_analysis()
    """
    Demonstrate sparse PCA on simulated genomic data
    Similar to gene expression analysis workflow
    """
    
    # Simulate gene expression data
    # 500 samples, 10000 genes, with pathway structure
    n_samples, n_genes = 500, 10000
    n_pathways = 20
    genes_per_pathway = 50
    
    # Generate pathway-structured data
    pathway_effects = randn(n_samples, n_pathways)
    
    X = zeros(n_samples, n_genes)
    pathway_memberships = []
    
    for i in 1:n_pathways
        gene_start = (i-1) * genes_per_pathway + 1
        gene_end = i * genes_per_pathway
        
        # Pathway loadings (some genes more influential)
        loadings = 0.1 * randn(genes_per_pathway) + 
                  [j <= 10 ? 1.0 : 0.2 for j in 1:genes_per_pathway]
        
        X[:, gene_start:gene_end] = pathway_effects[:, i:i] * loadings'
        push!(pathway_memberships, gene_start:gene_end)
    end
    
    # Add noise
    X += 0.5 * randn(n_samples, n_genes)
    
    # Apply sparse PCA to find pathway signatures
    S = cov(X)
    
    # Extract top 5 sparse components
    n_components = 5
    sparsity_level = 100  # Expect ~100 genes per component
    
    sparse_components = []
    for k in 1:n_components
        v_sparse, _ = sparse_pca_threshold(S, sparsity_level)
        push!(sparse_components, v_sparse)
        
        # Deflate matrix for next component
        S = S - (v_sparse' * S * v_sparse) * (v_sparse * v_sparse')
    end
    
    # Analyze pathway enrichment in components
    for (i, component) in enumerate(sparse_components)
        active_genes = findall(abs.(component) .> 1e-6)
        println("Component $i: $(length(active_genes)) active genes")
        
        # Check pathway enrichment
        for (j, pathway) in enumerate(pathway_memberships)
            overlap = length(intersect(active_genes, pathway))
            if overlap > 10  # Significant overlap
                println("  Enriched in pathway $j: $overlap/$(length(pathway)) genes")
            end
        end
    end
    
    return sparse_components, pathway_memberships
end

2.33.2 Imaging Biomarkers
